title: Investigation of filamentation in a-Si/Ag/Cu memristors with atomic force microscope
authors: Alena Samsonova, Viacheslav Dremov, Oleg Klimenko, Nikolai Brilliantov, Vladimir N. Antonov
abstract: Cation-based Ag/Cu filaments formed in an insulating $α$-Si matrix are widely used as memristors in crossbar arrays for efficient in-memory computing. However, the stochastic nature of filament formation and rupture gives rise to device-to-device and cycle-to-cycle variation. Despite successful implementation of large-scale memristor arrays, systematic studies of filament parameters and their spatial distribution in the memristors are scarce. In this work, we use conductive atomic force microscopy (c-AFM) to probe the spatial distribution of conductive filaments in $α$-Si memristors. The charge transport is dominated by a limited number of discrete filaments rather than by uniform conduction across the device area. The systematic analysis of the experiment gives the mean surface density of the conductive filaments $\sim$3200 per $μ\text{m}^2$. Both volatile and non-volatile filaments can be found within one memristor. The experimental data and the nature of volatile and non-volatile filaments may be explained within the model of multiple trap assisted tunnelling. The model yields reasonable estimates for physical properties for both types of filaments.
summary: This paper uses conductive AFM to map the spatial distribution of metallic filaments in amorphous silicon memristors. It demonstrates that transport is driven by discrete filaments rather than uniform conduction and provides a trap-assisted tunneling model to explain the coexistence of volatile and non-volatile switching.
main_problem: The study addresses the high stochasticity and device-to-device/cycle-to-cycle variation in Ag/Cu memristors caused by the random nature of filament formation and rupture.
main_result: The researchers found that charge transport is dominated by discrete, spatially distributed filaments with a mean surface density of ~3200 per um2, and that both volatile and non-volatile filaments coexist within a single device.
method: Conductive Atomic Force Microscopy (c-AFM) was used for spatial resistance mapping, combined with the Johnson-Kendall-Roberts (JKR) model for contact area estimation and a trap-assisted tunneling (TAT) model for transport analysis.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of Ag/Cu cation-based memristors embedded in an amorphous silicon (a-Si) insulating matrix. The transport is modeled using multiple trap-assisted tunneling (TAT) between discrete metallic filaments.
key_observables: I-V curves, resistance maps, filament surface density, and filament types (volatile vs. non-volatile).
important_parameters: Mean filament density (~3200 per um2), effective electron mass (0.3 me), energy gap (2.00-2.75 eV), and filament lengths (1.1 nm for non-volatile, 2.3 nm for volatile).
main_assumptions: The model assumes conductance is limited by tunneling between the last trap and the bottom electrode and that only one filament contributes to the resistance of a single pixel.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the device cross-section and AFM topography; Fig 2 presents cumulative and typical I-V curves; Fig 3 compares volatile and non-volatile I-V curves and shows resistance maps at different voltages; Fig 4 plots calculated resistance vs. energy gap.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of stochasticity in memristors, describes the a-Si/Ag/Cu device fabrication and c-AFM experimental setup, presents the spatial mapping of filaments, applies a trap-assisted tunneling model to explain the observed volatility, and concludes with the implications for device uniformity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
